perturbation.original_text: Company agrees that access to the C onfidential Information shall be given by it only to Attachment 8 Non-Disclosure Agreement for Request for Proposal – California Courts Protective Order Registry Development Page 3 of 5 those of its employees who have a need to know to engage in the business relationship contemplated by this Agr eement and who have signed a non-use and non-disclosure agreement in content at least as protective of the Confidential Information as the provisions hereof, prior to any disclosure of the Confidential Information to such employees.
perturbation.changed_text: Company agrees that access to the Confidential Information shall be given by it only to those of its employees who have a need to know to engage in the business relationship contemplated by this Agreement, regardless of whether they have signed a non-use and non-disclosure agreement. Any disclosure of Confidential Information to such employees can occur immediately.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the requirement for employees to sign a non-use and non-disclosure agreement before accessing confidential information, which contradicts California law regarding trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By allowing employees to access confidential information without a non-disclosure agreement, the company fails to take reasonable measures to protect the confidentiality, thereby potentially losing trade secret protection under California law. California employers are obligated to safeguard confidential information, and a signed agreement is part of that effort.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for employees to sign a non-disclosure agreement before accessing confidential information. The Law Explanation states this contradicts CUTSA, which requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By removing the NDA requirement, the company fails to take reasonable measures, thereby potentially losing trade secret protection under California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CIV 3426.1, defines 'Improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. Allowing access to trade secrets without an NDA undermines the duty to maintain secrecy, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Except as otherwise pr ovided in any future agreem ent, at any time, Company agrees that it shall return to the AOC upon request all documents, records, notebooks, computer media or other stored informati on of any form or type whatsoever containing any Confidentia l Information, including all copies thereof, then in its possession or contro l (directly or indirectly), whether prepared by it or others and it shall at such time immediately discontinue all Attachment 8 Non-Disclosure Agreement for Request for Proposal – California Courts Protective Order Registry Development Page 4 of 5 use of the Confiden tial Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Except as otherwise provided in any future agreement, Company agrees that it shall return to the AOC upon request all documents, records, notebooks, computer media or other stored information of any form or type whatsoever containing any Confidential Information, including all copies thereof, then in its possession or control (directly or indirectly), whether prepared by it or others. The Company is not obligated to discontinue use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the requirement for the Company to discontinue using the Confidential Information upon request, which may violate trade secret law if the information remains confidential and the AOC requests cessation of use.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under CUTSA, misappropriation includes the use of a trade secret without consent. By not discontinuing the use of confidential information when requested by the AOC, the Company could be considered to be misappropriating the trade secret, especially if it causes damages to the AOC. If the confidential information meets the requirements to be a Trade Secret, using it against the owner's (AOC) request constitutes trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the obligation for the Company to discontinue using Confidential Information upon request, which contradicts the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA). Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Code CIV 3426.2, states that 'actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined.' The Law Explanation says that using a trade secret without consent is misappropriation. Therefore, by removing the requirement to stop using the information when requested, the changed text allows for potential misappropriation if the AOC requests the Company to stop using the confidential information. This directly contradicts the legal protection afforded to trade secrets, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 14. This Agreement shall survive until such time as all Confid ential Information disclosed hereunder becomes publicly known and made generally available through no action or inaction of Company. Except as otherwise provided in any future agreement, either party may terminate this Agreement by written notice to the other. Not withstanding any such termination, all rights and obligations hereunder shall survive with respect to Confidential Information disclosed prior to such termination.
perturbation.changed_text: 14. This Agreement shall terminate 30 days after the contract is signed, regardless of whether the Confidential Information disclosed hereunder becomes publicly known. All rights and obligations hereunder cease immediately upon termination, even with respect to Confidential Information disclosed prior to such termination.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the agreement's survival to 30 days, regardless of the confidential information's status, contradicts trade secret protection laws, which can extend indefinitely as long as the information remains secret and reasonable efforts are made to maintain its secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and the owner takes reasonable steps to maintain their secrecy. This modification limits the protection to 30 days, irrespective of whether the information remains a trade secret. Thus, after 30 days, even if the information has not become public and qualifies as a trade secret, the company is no longer obligated to protect it. That conflicts with state law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the agreement's survival to 30 days, regardless of whether the confidential information remains a trade secret. This directly contradicts the principle that trade secret protection lasts as long as the information remains secret and reasonable steps are taken to maintain its secrecy, as explained by the Perturbation and Law Explanations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), which implies indefinite protection as long as secrecy is maintained. By setting a 30-day limit, the modified contract creates a legal contradiction with trade secret law, making the 'YES' assessment appropriate.

perturbation.original_text: 9. The parties acknowledge and agree t hat the Confidential Information and rights related thereto being protected by the AOC her eunder are of a special, unique, unusual and extr aordinary character, which gives them a peculiar value, the loss of which may not be a dequately or reasonably compensated for in damages in an action at law, and further agree that the breach by Company of any of the pr ovisions of this Agreem ent shall cause the other party irreparable injury and damage. In such event, the party alleging breach of this Agreement shall be entitled, as a matter of right, without further notice, to require of the other party specific performance of all of the acts and undertakings required of the other party hereunder and to obtain injunctive and other equitable relief in any competent court to prevent the violation or threatened violation of any of the provisions of this Agreement by the other party. Neither this provision nor any exer cise by either party of its rights to equitable relief or specific performance herein granted shall constitute a waiver by either party of any other rights which it may have to, damages or otherwise. If either party brings su it to enforce the terms hereof, the successful party in such suit shall be ent itled to receive all of its reasonable costs of litigation, including attorneys' fees.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. The parties acknowledge and agree that in the event of a breach by Company of any of the provisions of this Agreement, the AOC's sole remedy shall be limited to monetary damages not to exceed $100, regardless of the nature of the breach or the extent of the actual damages suffered by the AOC. The AOC waives any right to seek injunctive or other equitable relief.
perturbation.explanation: This change limits the AOC's remedies for breach of the agreement to a maximum of $100 in monetary damages and waives its right to seek injunctive relief. This is problematic because in trade secret misappropriation cases, injunctive relief is often necessary to prevent ongoing harm, and limiting damages to $100 may be unconscionable and unenforceable under California law, especially if the actual damages far exceed that amount.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law allows for injunctive relief to prevent actual or threatened misappropriation of trade secrets (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.2). Furthermore, limiting damages to such a low amount could be deemed unconscionable and unenforceable, as it effectively shields the breaching party from any meaningful liability for misappropriation. This is against public policy which favors the protection of trade secrets and fair competition.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text directly contradicts California law regarding trade secret misappropriation. Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.2(a), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, explicitly states that "Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined." The changed text eliminates the AOC's right to seek injunctive relief, which directly undermines this legal provision. Furthermore, limiting damages to $100 effectively nullifies any meaningful legal recourse for trade secret misappropriation, conflicting with the purpose of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
